sections.heading: Sector 1
sections.body:
We start our lap with an uphill run to turn 1, Niki Lauda Kurve. Take some of the kerb on the left to widen the corner as much as possible, hit the brakes at the 100 meter board and shift down to fourth. You want to be back on the throttle as early as possible, but watch the yellow bump on the inside kerb - that can unsettle your car. Take a bit of exit kerb to the left while minding potential wheelspin.

From here, its a flat out run up the hill on a not-quite-straight part of the track.

sections.heading: Sector 2
sections.body:
Approaching what is officially turn 3 but really only the second bend on the track, we brake at 110 meters for Remus Kurve, the slowest part of the track. Use some of the left-hand kerb to widen the turn again and shift all the way down to second gear. Try to get as close to the apex as possible, but not on the kerb in order to keep the car stable.

Avoid wheelspin by slowly easing back on the power and short shifting to get a good exit.The following straight starts going downhill towards the end.

The downhill Schlossgold turn is easy to run wide at - a patient right foot is important.

This means that for Schlossgold, probably the most technical of the Red Bull Rings corners, we need to brake a bit earlier than it would seem at first, namely at about 100 meters. Again, take some outside kerb on entry, shift down to third and use some of the apex kerb. Patience on the throttle is necessary, you can slowly ease back onto it as the corner starts to open up again. Run close to the outside edge of the track one exit, then transition to the right.

Turn 6 is called Rauch, and it is very fast - a short tap of the brakes at the 50 meter board allow you to pass through in fifth gear while either sticking to the inside kerb the entire way - or for more advanced drivers, stay on the outside a bit longer for a late apex. Run on the exit kerb and stay on it with your right-side wheels.

sections.heading: Sector 3
sections.body:
The following left-hander called Würth is a similar story - fifth gear, only a short stab on the brake, and then to turn in. You can use a fair bit of inside kerb, but be careful with the throttle if you do! Run wide on what is the inside kerb of the second part of the corner - a full-throttle right-hander - and take some exit kerb to the left of that as well - but mind the track limits, as youll get slapped with an invalidated lap here quite easily.

Over the crest and down the hill it goes towards Rindt, the penultimate corner. Despite being downhill, you can brake late at the 50 meter board, then turn in immediately and take a good chunk of inside kerb in fifth gear. Back on the throttle, you get carried to the outside kerb that you can use to widen the final turn, Red Bull Mobile, a little bit.

You can take some of the green kerb, too, but its playing with penalty fire, so to speak.

Start braking as you are alongside the pit entry, shift down to fourth and try to get as much of the apex as you can - easier said than done, as cars get carried to the outside easily here. Take some exit kerb, but be mindful not to go too far over it in order to avoid a penalty - and up the hill you go again for another lap at the Red Bull Ring.
